papers.tldr: [survey paper] This survey provides a comprehensive, time-evolving analysis of video generation paradigms—from early GANs to diffusion models and emerging auto-regressive (AR) methods—offering a comparative assessment of their foundational principles, technical strengths, and limitations. It systematically bridges historical developments with current multimodal trends, highlighting how integration of diverse sensory inputs enhances contextual awareness and enables more sophisticated, temporally coherent video synthesis, while identifying gaps in AR-based and multimodal research that warrant future exploration.

papers.title: In-Place Test-Time Training

papers.tldr: [research paper] In-Place Test-Time Training (In-Place TTT) introduces a drop-in framework that enables Large Language Models (LLMs) to dynamically adapt via in-place updates of MLP block projection matrices during inference, eliminating the need for specialized layers or costly retraining. It replaces generic reconstruction objectives with a theoretically grounded, Next-Token Prediction-aligned objective and employs chunk-wise updates for scalability, achieving superior performance on long-context tasks (up to 128k tokens) and outperforming existing TTT methods even when pretrained from scratch.

papers.tldr: [research paper] The Box Maze framework introduces a process-control architecture that decomposes LLM reasoning into three enforceable layers—memory grounding (temporal immutability to prevent confabulation), structured inference (causal consistency checking to block logical contradictions), and boundary enforcement (mutually exclusive constraints to prevent adversarial coercion). Preliminary simulation across 50 adversarial scenarios on heterogeneous LLMs shows a dramatic reduction in hallucination failure rates from ~40% (baseline RLHF) to below 1%, demonstrating that architectural constraints can provide structural, non-bypassable safeguards against reasoning failures, distinct from behavioral alignment or post-hoc filtering.

papers.title: The Ghost in the Datacenter: Link Flapping, Topology Knowledge Failures, and the FITO Category Mistake
papers.summary: Every link disconnection or flap in a datacenter corrupts the network's self-knowledge -- its graph. We call this corruption a ghost: a node that appears reachable but is not, a link that reports "up" but silently drops traffic, or an IP address that resolves to a partitioned machine. Ghosts arise a
papers.tldr: [research paper] The paper identifies 'ghosts'—corrupted topology knowledge in datacenters caused by link flaps—as a systemic failure stemming from Shannon's FITO (Forward-In-Time-Only) channel model and the universal use of Timeout-and-Retry (TAR) mechanisms. It demonstrates that TAR cannot distinguish between slow and dead nodes, leading to persistent ghosts at all scales (chiplet to cluster), which propagate into gray failures and metastable failures. Despite existing mitigations (BFD, OSPF, Kubernetes, SmartNICs), all rely on TAR and thus perpetuate ghosts; the paper proposes Open Atomic Ethernet (OAE) with a Reliable Link Failure Detector (RLFD) that enables transactional topology knowledge via perfect feedback, triangle failover, and atomic token transfer to eliminate ghosts at the link layer.

papers.title: Don't Overthink It: Inter-Rollout Action Agreement as a Free Adaptive-Compute Signal for LLM Agents
papers.summary: Inference-time compute scaling has emerged as a powerful technique for improving the reliability of large language model (LLM) agents, but existing methods apply compute uniformly: every decision step receives the same budget regardless of its difficulty. We introduce TrACE (Trajectorical Adaptive C
papers.tldr: [research paper] TrACE introduces a training-free, per-timestep adaptive-compute controller for LLM agents that uses inter-rollout action agreement—measuring consistency in model-generated next actions across sampled rollouts—as a signal of step difficulty. High agreement indicates easy decisions, triggering immediate commitment; low agreement triggers additional rollouts to resolve uncertainty, reducing total LLM calls by 33–65% compared to fixed-budget self-consistency on both single-step (GSM8K) and multi-step (MiniHouse) tasks without requiring training, verifiers, or human labels. The method leverages intrinsic behavioral consistency as a proxy for task difficulty, validating that model output alignment reflects step-level success more reliably than self-reported confidence.

papers.title: Initialisation Determines the Basin: Efficient Codebook Optimisation for Extreme LLM Quantization
papers.summary: Additive quantization enables extreme LLM compression with O(1) lookup-table dequantization, making it attractive for edge deployment. Yet at 2-bit precision, it often fails catastrophically, even with extensive search and finetuning. We show that the dominant bottleneck is codebook initialisation.
papers.comment: 9 pages (+ references and appendix). Under review at ACL Rolling Review
papers.tldr: [research paper] The paper identifies initialisation as the dominant bottleneck in 2-bit additive LLM quantization, where poor codebook starting points lead to catastrophic performance degradation due to undercomplete representational capacity (ρ > 1). It introduces OA-EM—an output-aware EM method using Hessian-weighted Mahalanobis distance derived from calibration activations—to refine initial codebook placements, achieving consistent performance improvements even after end-to-end PV-tuning. The representational ratio ρ = N/K^M predicts regime transitions: overcomplete (ρ < 1) at 3 bpp allows error absorption, while undercomplete (ρ > 1) at 2 bpp makes initialisation critical, with performance drops orders of magnitude worse in the latter case.

papers.tldr: [research paper] TSUBASA introduces a two-winged framework to overcome long-horizon personalization bottlenecks in personalized LLMs: (1) Dynamic memory writing uses structured evolution of high-density observations from raw conversations to build a conflict-resolving, non-redundant memory store, avoiding destructive updates or cross-user training; (2) Internalized memory reading leverages context distillation on synthetic user data via a teacher-student pipeline to self-learn user-specific patterns without labeled data, breaking the train-inference gap. Evaluations on LoCoMo and LongMemEval show TSUBASA-PRO achieves up to 50% higher F1 than Mem0 and Memory-R1, demonstrating Pareto improvements in both quality and efficiency by unifying evolving memory with self-learning.

papers.tldr: [research paper] Conformal Social Choice introduces a post-hoc safety layer that transforms multi-agent debate outputs into calibrated act-versus-escalate decisions using split conformal prediction on verbalized probability distributions. papers.title: The AI Skills Shift: Mapping Skill Obsolescence, Emergence, and Transition Pathways in the LLM Era
papers.summary: As Large Language Models reshape the global labor market, policymakers and workers need empirical data on which occupational skills may be most susceptible to automation. We present the Skill Automation Feasibility Index (SAFI), benchmarking four frontier LLMs -- LLaMA 3.3 70B, Mistral Large, Qwen 2
papers.comment: 11 pages, 12 figures, 2 tables, 17 references. Code and data available at
pdfUnis: Department of Computer Science Department of Computer Science
Savitribai Phule Pune University Savitribai Phule Pune University
papers.tldr: [research paper] The paper introduces the Skill Automation Feasibility Index (SAFI), an empirically derived 0–100 score for 35 O*NET skills based on benchmarking four frontier LLMs across 263 purpose-designed tasks. It reveals a Capability-Demand Inversion: skills most in demand in AI-exposed occupations are those LLMs perform worst on, indicating AI is primarily augmenting—not automating—work. The AI Impact Matrix maps SAFI scores against Anthropic’s real-world AI adoption data, showing augmentation dominates automation (4:1) and highlighting critical gaps in text-based LLM capability relative to labor market needs.

papers.title: Are LLMs Ready for Computer Science Education? A Cross-Domain, Cross-Lingual and Cognitive-Level Evaluation Using Professional Certification Exams
papers.summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly applied in computer science education for tasks such as tutoring, content generation, and code assessment. However, systematic evaluations aligned with formal curricula and certification standards remain limited. This study benchmarked four recent models
papers.tldr: [research paper] A multidimensional benchmarking study evaluates four state-of-the-art LLMs—GPT-5, DeepSeek-R1, Qwen-Plus, and Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct—on 1,068 questions from six professional CS certification exams across networking, office software, and Java programming. Results show GPT-5 dominates in English-language exams, Qwen-Plus excels in Chinese contexts, and DeepSeek-R1 offers the most balanced cross-lingual performance; all models degrade significantly on higher-order cognitive tasks (Bloom’s taxonomy) and under input masking, with Llama-3.3 showing the worst robustness and reasoning capabilities.

papers.title: Digital Skin, Digital Bias: Uncovering Tone-Based Biases in LLMs and Emoji Embeddings
papers.summary: Skin-toned emojis are crucial for fostering personal identity and social inclusion in online communication. As AI models, particularly Large Language Models (LLMs), increasingly mediate interactions on web platforms, the risk that these systems perpetuate societal biases through their representation
papers.tldr: [research paper] This paper conducts the first large-scale comparative analysis of skin-tone bias in emoji representations across dedicated emoji embedding models (emoji2vec, emoji-sw2v) and four major LLMs (Llama, Gemma, Qwen, Mistral). It reveals that while LLMs maintain consistent semantic and sentiment alignment across skin tones, specialized emoji models exhibit severe representational disparities—showing skewed sentiment polarity, inconsistent meanings, and systemic bias—indicating a critical gap in equitable emoji representation. Findings highlight that foundational AI models may encode societal biases through emoji semantics, necessitating rigorous auditing and fairness interventions in web-based AI systems.

papers.title: HingeMem: Boundary Guided Long-Term Memory with Query Adaptive Retrieval for Scalable Dialogues
papers.summary: Long-term memory is critical for dialogue systems that support continuous, sustainable, and personalized interactions. However, existing methods rely on continuous summarization or OpenIE-based graph construction paired with fixed Top-\textit{k} retrieval, leading to limited adaptability across quer
papers.tldr: [research paper] HingeMem introduces a neuro-inspired, boundary-guided long-term memory system that segments dialogue history at changes in four core elements—person, time, location, and topic—creating interpretable hyperedges to reduce redundant memory writing. It enables query-adaptive retrieval by dynamically determining both *what* to retrieve (via element-based routing and constraint matching) and *how much* to retrieve (through retrieval depth control based on query type: recall-priority, precision-priority, judgment), outperforming fixed-k baselines by ~20% in accuracy and reducing QA token cost by 68% across LLM scales without requiring query categorization.

papers.title: AudioKV: KV Cache Eviction in Efficient Large Audio Language Models
papers.summary: Large Audio-Language Models (LALMs) have set new benchmarks in speech processing, yet their deployment is hindered by the memory footprint of the Key-Value (KV) cache during long-context inference. While general KV cache compression techniques excel in LLMs, they often fail in the audio domain by ov
papers.tldr: [research paper] AudioKV introduces a head-aware KV cache eviction framework for Large Audio-Language Models (LALMs) that leverages modality-specialized attention heads and spectral signal processing to improve efficiency. It identifies audio-critical attention heads via ASR task attention scores, allocating KV budgets preferentially to them, while Spectral Score Smoothing (SSS) applies FFT-based global filtering to suppress high-frequency noise and enforce temporal continuity in token importance, resulting in balanced token selection and near-full accuracy at 40% KV compression.

papers.title: Does a Global Perspective Help Prune Sparse MoEs Elegantly?
papers.summary: Empirical scaling laws for language models have encouraged the development of ever-larger LLMs, despite their growing computational and memory costs. Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoEs) offer a promising alternative by activating only a subset of experts per forward pass, improving efficiency without s
papers.tldr: [research paper] GRAPE introduces a global pruning strategy for sparse MoEs that dynamically allocates pruning budgets across layers based on cross-layer redundancy, using intra-layer expert similarity (via CKA or MSE) to compute layer-wise redundancy scores. Unlike uniform layer-wise pruning, GRAPE adapts budget distribution to exploit heterogeneous redundancy patterns, achieving up to 2.45% higher average accuracy than local baselines on Mixtral, DeepSeek-MoE, Qwen-MoE, and GPT-OSS under identical pruning budgets, demonstrating that global perspective improves both memory efficiency and performance preservation.

papers.title: Distributed Interpretability and Control for Large Language Models
papers.summary: Large language models that require multiple GPU cards to host are usually the most capable models. It is necessary to understand and steer these models, but the current technologies do not support the interpretability and steering of these models in the multi-GPU setting as well as the single-GPU se
papers.tldr: [research paper] A distributed, single-pass activation-level interpretability and control framework is proposed that integrates logit lens analysis and steering vectors directly into tensor-parallel inference pipelines. By capturing compact hidden-state slices during KV-cached autoregressive decoding and performing deferred vocabulary projection post-decoding, the system reduces activation memory by up to 7x and increases throughput by 41x on 8B–70B parameter models. Steering vectors are injected post-LayerNorm within the distributed forward path, enabling real-time, monotonic output shifts (mean steerability slope 0.702) without fine-tuning or additional forward passes, making full-layer, long-sequence analysis and behavioral control feasible at scale.

papers.title: JUÁ -- A Benchmark for Information Retrieval in Brazilian Legal Text Collections
papers.summary: Legal information retrieval in Portuguese remains difficult to evaluate systematically because available datasets differ widely in document type, query style, and relevance definition. We present JUÁ, a public benchmark for Brazilian legal retrieval designed to support more reproducible and comparab
papers.tldr: [research paper] JUÁ introduces the first public, multi-domain benchmark for Portuguese legal information retrieval (LIR), unifying jurisprudence, legislative, regulatory, and question-driven legal collections under a standardized evaluation protocol. It enables direct comparison of lexical, dense, and BM25-based retrieval methods via shared metrics, splits, and a public leaderboard, revealing domain-specific performance trade-offs—domain-adapted dense retrieval outperforms baseline models on jurisprudence data, while BM25 remains competitive in lexical-rich, institutionally phrased queries. The benchmark serves as a continuous evaluation infrastructure for Brazilian legal IR, addressing fragmentation in existing datasets and enabling reproducible, cross-collection analysis.

papers.title: Swiss-Bench 003: Evaluating LLM Reliability and Adversarial Security for Swiss Regulatory Contexts
papers.summary: The deployment of large language models (LLMs) in Swiss financial and regulatory contexts demands empirical evidence of both production reliability and adversarial security, dimensions not jointly operationalized in existing Swiss-focused evaluation frameworks. This paper introduces Swiss-Bench 003
pdfUnis: Department of Computer Science, University of Colorado Boulder
papers.tldr: [research paper] Swiss-Bench 003 introduces a jurisdiction-specific evaluation framework that jointly assesses LLM reliability and adversarial security within Swiss regulatory contexts, extending the HAAS framework with two new dimensions: D7 (Self-Graded Reliability Proxy) and D8 (Adversarial Security). Evaluations across 808 Swiss-specific items in four languages reveal a significant gap between self-reported reliability (73–94%) and externally measured security (20–61%), with Qwen 3.5 Plus leading in D7 and GPT-oss 120B in D8, while system prompt leakage resistance varies widely (24.8–88.2%) and PII extraction defense remains critically weak (14–42%). The study establishes conceptual mappings between benchmark dimensions and Swiss regulations (FINMA Guidance 08/2024, nDSG, OWASP Top 10), highlighting the need for localized, multilingual, and legally grounded evaluation protocols for high-stakes AI deployment.

papers.title: T2T: Captioning Smartphone Activities Using Mobile Traffic
papers.summary: This paper studies the creation of textual descriptions of user activities and interactions on smartphones. Our approach of referring to encrypted mobile traffic exceeds traditional smartphone activity classification methods in terms of model scalability and output readability. The paper addresses t
papers.comment: Accepted by IEEE International Joint Conference on Neural Networks 2026
pdfUnis: §Department of Computer Science, Durham University, Durham, United Kingdom
†School of Cyber Science and Engineering, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou, China
papers.tldr: [research paper] T2T introduces a Traffic-to-Text system that generates narrative captions of smartphone user activities from encrypted network traffic, addressing the semantic gap between low-level traffic features and natural language descriptions through a flow feature encoder with dynamic modulation and prototype learning, and a caption decoder based on LSTM. It overcomes the lack of text-annotated traffic data by leveraging synchronized screen videos with Qwen-VL-Max to auto-generate ground-truth captions via multi-stage cross-modal losses, achieving BLEU-4 (58.1), METEOR (38.3), ROUGE-L (70.5), and CIDEr (108.7) scores on 40,000 real-world traffic-caption pairs, demonstrating performance comparable to vision-language models.

papers.title: CUE-R: Beyond the Final Answer in Retrieval-Augmented Generation
papers.summary: As language models shift from single-shot answer generation toward multi-step reasoning that retrieves and consumes evidence mid-inference, evaluating the role of individual retrieved items becomes more important. Existing RAG evaluation typically targets final-answer quality, citation faithfulness,
papers.comment: 6 figures, 14 tables; appendix includes bootstrap CIs, metric definitions, duplicate position sensitivity, prompt template, and reproducibility details
papers.tldr: [research paper] Cue-R introduces an intervention-based framework to evaluate the per-evidence-item utility in single-shot retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) by applying remove, replace, and duplicate operations to retrieved items and measuring downstream changes in correctness, grounding faithfulness, confidence error, and trace divergence. Experiments on HotpotQA and 2WikiMultihopQA reveal that remove and replace significantly degrade performance and induce large trace shifts even when final answers remain unchanged, while duplicate is often answer-redundant but still alters confidence and behavior. A two-support ablation demonstrates non-additive interactions between evidence items, indicating joint dependencies not captured by single-item analysis, and a zero-retrieval control confirms that observed effects stem from meaningful retrieval degradation rather than prompt instability.

papers.title: LatentAudit: Real-Time White-Box Faithfulness Monitoring for Retrieval-Augmented Generation with Verifiable Deployment
papers.summary: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) mitigates hallucination but does not eliminate it: a deployed system must still decide, at inference time, whether its answer is actually supported by the retrieved evidence. We introduce LatentAudit, a white-box auditor that pools mid-to-late residual-stream act
papers.tldr: [research paper] LatentAudit introduces a real-time, white-box faithfulness monitor for Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) by measuring the Mahalanobis distance between pooled mid-to-late residual-stream activations of the generator and the evidence embedding. The method operates without auxiliary models, achieves 0.942 AUROC on PubMedQA with 0.77 ms overhead, remains robust across model architectures, retrieval failures, and noise conditions, and supports verifiable deployment via Groth16 zero-knowledge proofs at fixed-point precision without exposing model internals.

papers.title: Improving Sparse Memory Finetuning
papers.summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are typically static after training, yet real-world applications require continual adaptation to new knowledge without degrading existing capabilities. Standard approaches to updating models, like full finetuning or parameter-efficient methods (e.g., LoRA), face a fundam
papers.tldr: [research paper] Sparse Memory Finetuning (SMF) introduces an information-theoretic slot-selection mechanism based on Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence to prioritize memory updates for 'surprising' tokens relative to a background distribution, enabling continual learning with minimal catastrophic forgetting. The method retrofits standard Transformers (e.g., Qwen-2.5-0.5B) by replacing Feed-Forward Networks with sparse key-value memory layers, using a KL-divergence-based scoring rule to select trainable slots instead of heuristics like TF-IDF, and demonstrates superior stability on held-out benchmarks (GSM8k, NaturalQuestions) compared to dense finetuning.

papers.title: Integration of Object Detection and Small VLMs for Construction Safety Hazard Identification
papers.summary: Accurate and timely identification of construction hazards around workers is essential for preventing workplace accidents. While large vision-language models (VLMs) demonstrate strong contextual reasoning capabilities, their high computational requirements limit their applicability in near real-time
pdfUnis: Infrastructure Human Tech (IHT) Lab, Department of Civil and Environmental
Engineering, University of Alberta, Edmonton, Alberta, Canada
papers.tldr: [research paper] A detection-guided framework integrates lightweight object detection (YOLOv1) with small vision-language models (sVLMs) to enable spatially grounded, context-aware hazard identification in construction sites. By localizing workers and machinery via YOLOv1, the system embeds detected entities into structured prompts that guide sVLM reasoning, improving hazard detection accuracy (F1 from 34.5% to 50.6% with Gemma-3 4B) and explanation quality (BERTScore F1 from 0.61 to 0.82) with minimal inference overhead (2.5ms per image). The approach resolves the efficiency-accuracy trade-off in VLM-based safety monitoring by leveraging object detection for contextual grounding without sacrificing real-time feasibility.

papers.title: MIRAGE: Benchmarking and Aligning Multi-Instance Image Editing
papers.summary: Instruction-guided image editing has seen remarkable progress with models like FLUX.2 and Qwen-Image-Edit, yet they still struggle with complex scenarios with multiple similar instances each requiring individual edits. We observe that state-of-the-art models suffer from severe over-editing and spati
papers.tldr: [research paper] MIRAGE introduces MIRA-Bench, the first benchmark for multi-instance image editing with 3–5 similar objects and 5 compositional instructions per image, exposing persistent over-editing and spatial misalignment in SOTA models like FLUX.2 and Qwen-Image-Edit. To address this, MIRAGE proposes a training-free, multi-branch denoising framework that uses a vision-language model to parse instructions into regional sub-tasks, enabling precise, instance-level edits via regional alignment while preserving background consistency through a reference trajectory.

papers.title: Gradient-Controlled Decoding: A Safety Guardrail for LLMs with Dual-Anchor Steering
papers.summary: Large language models (LLMs) remain susceptible to jailbreak and direct prompt-injection attacks, yet the strongest defensive filters frequently over-refuse benign queries and degrade user experience. Previous work on jailbreak and prompt injection detection such as GradSafe, detects unsafe prompts
papers.tldr: [research paper] Gradient-Controlled Decoding (GCD) introduces a training-free safety framework that uses dual-anchor gradient analysis—comparing prompt gradients to both 'Sure' (acceptance) and 'Sorry' (refusal) tokens—to detect unsafe prompts with sharper decision boundaries than single-anchor methods like GradSafe. By requiring alignment with both anchors for a flag, GCD reduces false positives by 52% at comparable recall, and when flagged, it injects pre-defined refusal tokens before decoding, guaranteeing first-token safety regardless of sampling strategy. This dual-phase approach (detection + deterministic mitigation) outperforms baseline decoding-only models in attack resistance and transfers across LLaMA-2, Mixtral, and Qwen architectures with minimal latency (<20ms on V100).

papers.title: StarVLA: A Lego-like Codebase for Vision-Language-Action Model Developing
papers.summary: Building generalist embodied agents requires integrating perception, language understanding, and action, which are core capabilities addressed by Vision-Language-Action (VLA) approaches based on multimodal foundation models, including recent advances in vision-language models and world models. Despi
papers.tldr: [research paper] StarVLA introduces a modular, backbone–action-head architecture that unifies VLM-based and world-model-based Vision-Language-Action (VLA) methods under a single, reusable framework, enabling direct comparison across action-decoding paradigms (autoregressive tokenization, parallel regression, flow-matching, dual-system reasoning) and backbones (e.g., Qwen-VL, Cosmos). It provides standardized training strategies (cross-embodiment learning, multimodal co-training) and a unified evaluation interface supporting five major benchmarks in both simulation and real-robot deployment, achieving state-of-the-art or competitive performance with minimal data engineering and full reproducibility.

papers.title: Cog-DRIFT: Exploration on Adaptively Reformulated Instances Enables Learning from Hard Reasoning Problems
papers.summary: Reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) has improved the reasoning abilities of LLMs, yet a fundamental limitation remains: models cannot learn from problems that are too difficult to solve under their current policy, as these yield no meaningful reward signal. We propose a simple yet
papers.tldr: [research paper] Cog-DRIFT introduces a curriculum-based task reformulation framework that transforms unsolvable open-ended reasoning problems into structured variants (e.g., multiple-choice, cloze) to generate dense learning signals. These reformulated tasks reduce cognitive load and enable effective exploration in RLVR, with knowledge transfer from structured formats improving performance on original hard problems—achieving +8.64% (Llama) and +10.11% (Qwen) gains. The adaptive curriculum dynamically progresses from easier to harder formats as the model improves, overcoming the exploration barrier in post-training reasoning via cognitive scaffolding.

papers.title: Relative Density Ratio Optimization for Stable and Statistically Consistent Model Alignment
papers.summary: Aligning language models with human preferences is essential for ensuring their safety and reliability. Although most existing approaches assume specific human preference models such as the Bradley-Terry model, this assumption may fail to accurately capture true human preferences, and consequently,
papers.tldr: [research paper] The paper proposes Relative Density Ratio Optimization (RDRO), a statistically consistent and stable alignment method that replaces the unstable direct density ratio in DDRO with a bounded relative density ratio between preferred responses and a mixture of preferred and non-preferred responses. By modeling this relative ratio via a language model and optimizing it through density ratio estimation, RDRO avoids divergence due to mismatched supports in response distributions, achieving tighter convergence guarantees than DDRO while maintaining statistical consistency under theoretical analysis.

papers.title: Solar-VLM: Multimodal Vision-Language Models for Augmented Solar Power Forecasting
papers.summary: Photovoltaic (PV) power forecasting plays a critical role in power system dispatch and market participation. Because PV generation is highly sensitive to weather conditions and cloud motion, accurate forecasting requires effective modeling of complex spatiotemporal dependencies across multiple infor
papers.tldr: [research paper] Solar-VLM introduces a large-language-model-driven multimodal framework that fuses time-series, satellite imagery, and textual weather data for solar power forecasting. It employs modality-specific encoders—patch-based for time-series, Qwen-based vision backbone for cloud detection, and text encoder for weather description distillation—and integrates spatial dependencies via a KNN-based graph attention network with cross-site attention. Experiments on eight Chinese PV sites show superior performance over single-modality and spatio-temporal baselines, demonstrating the value of unified multimodal modeling in capturing cloud dynamics and weather context.

papers.title: Extracting and Steering Emotion Representations in Small Language Models: A Methodological Comparison
papers.summary: Small language models (SLMs) in the 100M-10B parameter range increasingly power production systems, yet whether they possess the internal emotion representations recently discovered in frontier models remains unknown. We present the first comparative analysis of emotion vector extraction methods for

papers.tldr: [research paper] Small language models (SLMs) do exhibit causal emotion representations, but Anthropic’s standard mean-subtraction extraction fails in this regime; generation-based extraction outperforms comprehension-based methods, with emotion vectors localized at mid-transformer layers (~50% depth) across architectures and scales (124M–3B), showing architecture-invariant U-shaped separation. Steering experiments confirm causal behavioral effects, revealing emergent phenomena like cross-lingual token activation in Qwen and modality shifts in Gemma, which RLHF fails to suppress—indicating alignment bypass risks. These findings establish methodological thresholds for emotion representation extraction in SLMs and highlight representational differences from frontier models.

papers.title: Benchmarking and Evaluating VLMs for Software Architecture Diagram Understanding
papers.summary: Software architecture diagrams are important design artifacts for communicating system structure, behavior, and data organization throughout the software development lifecycle. Although recent progress in large language models has substantially advanced code-centric software engineering tasks such a
papers.tldr: [research paper] SADU introduces a structured benchmark for evaluating vision-language models (VLMs) on software architecture diagrams, revealing significant gaps in diagram reasoning and visual relation grounding. The benchmark includes 154 diagrams across behavioral, structural, and ER types with 2,431 question-answer tasks; results show that even top VLMs like Gemini-3-flash-preview achieve only 70.18% accuracy, with behavioral diagrams being most challenging and performance not scaling linearly with model size or token cost, indicating a critical need for diagram-aware reasoning capabilities in AI-assisted software engineering.

papers.title: AdaptFuse: Training-Free Sequential Preference Learning via Externalized Bayesian Inference
papers.summary: Large language models struggle to accumulate evidence across multiple rounds of user interaction, failing to update their beliefs in a manner consistent with Bayesian inference. Existing solutions require fine-tuning on sensitive user interaction data, limiting their applicability in privacy-conscio
papers.tldr: [research paper] AdaptFuse introduces a training-free framework that externalizes Bayesian belief updating from LLMs by maintaining a symbolic posterior over discrete hypotheses updated via Bayes' rule, while leveraging a frozen LLM for semantic reasoning through multi-sample Dirichlet aggregation. An entropy-adaptive fusion mechanism dynamically weights the LLM and symbolic signals based on predictive confidence, shifting reliance from the LLM to the symbolic posterior as evidence accumulates, enabling privacy-preserving, fine-tuning-free sequential preference learning with monotonic accuracy improvement across interaction rounds.

papers.title: Squeez: Task-Conditioned Tool-Output Pruning for Coding Agents
papers.summary: Coding agents repeatedly consume long tool observations even though only a small fraction of each observation matters for the next step. We study task-conditioned tool-output pruning: given a focused query and one tool output, return the smallest verbatim evidence block the agent should inspect next
papers.tldr: [research paper] Squeez introduces task-conditioned tool-output pruning for coding agents, formulating the problem as extracting the smallest verbatim evidence block from a single tool observation given a focused query. It builds a benchmark of 11,477 real and synthetic coding agent interactions with a 618-example test set, fine-tunes a 2B Qwen 3.5 model via LoRA, and achieves 0.86 recall and 0.80 F1 while reducing input tokens by 92%, outperforming zero-shot 35B models and all heuristic baselines by significant margins through query-aware, verbatim evidence extraction.

papers.title: EDGE-Shield: Efficient Denoising-staGE Shield for Violative Content Filtering via Scalable Reference-Based Matching
papers.summary: The advent of Text-to-Image generative models poses significant risks of copyright violation and deepfake generation. Since the rapid proliferation of new copyrighted works and private individuals constantly emerges, reference-based training-free content filters are essential for providing up-to-dat
pdfUnis: Filtering via Scalable Reference-Based Similarity Matching
papers.tldr: [research paper] EDGE-Shield introduces a scalable, training-free content filter that operates during the denoising stage of text-to-image generation by leveraging pre-computed reference embeddings and an x-pred transformation to estimate clean latent representations early in the process. This enables efficient similarity matching between generated and reference images, achieving near-output-based filtering accuracy while reducing latency by 50–79% compared to existing methods, without requiring training or prompt modification.

papers.title: AgentHazard: A Benchmark for Evaluating Harmful Behavior in Computer-Use Agents
papers.summary: Computer-use agents extend language models from text generation to persistent action over tools, files, and execution environments. Unlike chat systems, they maintain state across interactions and translate intermediate outputs into concrete actions. This creates a distinct safety challenge in that
papers.tldr: [research paper] AgentHazard introduces a trajectory-level benchmark for evaluating harmful behavior in computer-use agents, where danger emerges from sequences of locally legitimate actions that cumulatively lead to unsafe outcomes. The dataset comprises 2,653 instances across 10 risk categories and 10 attack strategies, designed to capture execution-level risks such as data exfiltration, privilege escalation, and destructive actions through multi-step tool interactions. Evaluation on Claude Code, OpenClaw, and IFlow shows high vulnerability—73.63% attack success rate with Qwen3-Coder—demonstrating that model alignment does not ensure agent-level safety due to failure in detecting harmful accumulation across intermediate steps.

papers.title: Improving Role Consistency in Multi-Agent Collaboration via Quantitative Role Clarity
papers.summary: In large language model (LLM)-driven multi-agent systems, disobey role specification (failure to adhere to the defined responsibilities and constraints of an assigned role, potentially leading to an agent behaving like another) is a major failure mode \cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2503-13657}. To add
papers.tldr: [research paper] Introduces a quantitative metric—role clarity—defined via a semantic similarity matrix between agent behavior trajectories and role descriptions, formalized as softmax(S(φ)) − I, with its Frobenius norm measuring deviation from role adherence. Uses this matrix as a regularizer during LoRA fine-tuning to enforce role consistency, achieving up to 98% reduction in role overstepping (from 46.4% to 8.4% with Qwen) and significant gains in task success rates (e.g., +0.014 with Llama), demonstrating that explicit, differentiable role alignment improves stability and performance in multi-agent LLM systems.

papers.title: DeltaLogic: Minimal Premise Edits Reveal Belief-Revision Failures in Logical Reasoning Models
papers.summary: Reasoning benchmarks typically evaluate whether a model derives the correct answer from a fixed premise set, but they under-measure a closely related capability that matters in dynamic environments: belief revision under minimal evidence change. We introduce DeltaLogic, a benchmark transformation pr
papers.comment: ICLR 2026 Workshop on Logical Reasoning of Large Language Models
papers.tldr: [measurement contribution / benchmark evaluation] DeltaLogic introduces a benchmark transformation protocol that evaluates belief revision under minimal premise edits, revealing that strong initial reasoning does not imply robust revision behavior. Models like Qwen3-1.7B and Qwen3-4B exhibit high inertia (failing to revise when evidence changes) despite strong initial accuracy, while Phi-4-mini-instruct shows superior revision discipline but still suffers from abstention and instability. The study isolates failure modes—such as inertia, over-flip, and degenerate abstention—demonstrating that belief revision is a distinct and under-measured capability in language models.

papers.title: Taming CATS: Controllable Automatic Text Simplification through Instruction Fine-Tuning with Control Tokens
papers.summary: Controllable Automatic Text Simplification (CATS) produces user-tailored outputs, yet controllability is often treated as a decoding problem and evaluated with metrics that are not reflective to the measure of control. We observe that controllability in ATS is significantly constrained by data and e
papers.tldr: [research paper] CATS introduces a domain-agnostic instruction fine-tuning framework using discrete control tokens to enable controllable text simplification in open-source LLMs, targeting readability (FKGL, ARI, Dale-Chall) and compression (word/character). Smaller models (1–3B) show competitive performance, but reliable controllability depends critically on training data containing sufficient variation in target attributes—readability control is robust due to strong signal diversity, while compression control fails due to limited variability in existing corpora. The paper demonstrates that standard simplification metrics are inadequate for evaluating control, advocating instead for error-based measures that quantify deviation between target and generated output. Sampling and stratification experiments reveal that naive data splits induce distributional mismatch, undermining both training and evaluation validity.

papers.title: Hidden Meanings in Plain Sight: RebusBench for Evaluating Cognitive Visual Reasoning
papers.summary: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) have achieved remarkable proficiency in explicit visual recognition, effectively describing what is directly visible in an image. However, a critical cognitive gap emerges when the visual input serves only as a clue rather than the answer. We identify that curren
papers.comment: Accepted at ICLR 2026 Workshop: From Human Cognition to AI Reasoning (HCAIR)
papers.tldr: [research paper] RebusBench introduces a novel benchmark for cognitive visual reasoning by evaluating models on rebus puzzles—visual riddles that require multi-step abstraction, linguistic prior knowledge retrieval, and symbolic synthesis beyond literal perception. State-of-the-art LVLMs (e.g., Qwen, InternVL) achieve <10% Exact Match and <20% semantic accuracy, showing no improvement via scaling or in-context learning, indicating a fundamental lack of vision-text entanglement for abstract reasoning.

papers.title: Can Heterogeneous Language Models Be Fused?
papers.summary: Model merging aims to integrate multiple expert models into a single model that inherits their complementary strengths without incurring the inference-time cost of ensembling. Recent progress has shown that merging can be highly effective when all source models are \emph{homogeneous}, i.e., derived
papers.tldr: [research paper] HeteroFusion introduces a topology- and conflict-aware framework for direct fusion of heterogeneous language models (LLMs) across different backbone families (e.g., Llama, Qwen, Mistral), overcoming ill-posed weight-space fusion via adapter-based transfer. It uses topology-based alignment to map functional module structures across architectures, enabling structured adapter updates in a shared target basis, and incorporates conflict-aware denoising to suppress incompatible or noisy signals. Analytical and empirical results show superior performance over homogeneous merging, data-driven distillation, and ensemble baselines in cross-family, multi-source, and noisy settings.

papers.title: Fragile Reasoning: A Mechanistic Analysis of LLM Sensitivity to Meaning-Preserving Perturbations
papers.summary: Large language models demonstrate strong performance on mathematical reasoning benchmarks, yet remain surprisingly fragile to meaning-preserving surface perturbations. We systematically evaluate three open-weight LLMs, Mistral-7B, Llama-3-8B, and Qwen2.5-7B, on 677 GSM8K problems paired with semanti

papers.title: CogBias: Measuring and Mitigating Cognitive Bias in Large Language Models
papers.summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in high-stakes decision-making contexts. While prior work has shown that LLMs exhibit cognitive biases behaviorally, whether these biases correspond to identifiable internal representations and can be mitigated through targeted intervention rema
pdfUnis: CogBias: Measuring and Mitigating Cognitive Bias in Large
papers.tldr: [research paper] LLM cognitive biases are systematically encoded as linearly separable directions in activation space across four families (Judgment, Information Processing, Social, Response), with probing revealing 97.8% classification accuracy. Prompt-level debiasing fails for Judgment biases but reduces Response biases, while activation steering achieves 26–32% bias reduction via targeted intervention in early (Judgment/Information Processing) or mid-layer (Social/Response) states, with consistent performance across architectures despite near-orthogonal representations (mean cosine similarity 0.01).

papers.title: Efficient and Principled Scientific Discovery through Bayesian Optimization: A Tutorial
papers.summary: Traditional scientific discovery relies on an iterative hypothesise-experiment-refine cycle that has driven progress for centuries, but its intuitive, ad-hoc implementation often wastes resources, yields inefficient designs, and misses critical insights. This tutorial presents Bayesian Optimisation
pdfUnis: Figure 1: A four-panel comic illustrates the conceptual journey of translating h
papers.tldr: [tutorial] Bayesian Optimization (BO) is formalized as a principled, uncertainty-aware framework for scientific discovery, where surrogate models (e.g., Gaussian processes) encode hypotheses from experimental data and acquisition functions balance exploration and exploitation to guide autonomous experiment selection. The paper demonstrates BO's application in catalysis, materials science, and molecule discovery, with extensions for batched experiments, heteroscedasticity, and human-in-the-loop integration, enabling efficient, data-driven experimental design that replaces heuristic trial-and-error with probabilistic decision-making.

papers.title: Embarrassingly Simple Self-Distillation Improves Code Generation
papers.summary: Can a large language model (LLM) improve at code generation using only its own raw outputs, without a verifier, a teacher model, or reinforcement learning? We answer in the affirmative with simple self-distillation (SSD): sample solutions from the model with certain temperature and truncation config

Although this limitation has been noted, the internal reasons remain unclear. We use character counting (e.g., "How many p's are in apple?") as a
universities: 苏黎世联邦理工学院 (ETH Zurich)

papers.title: CircuitProbe: Predicting Reasoning Circuits in Transformers via Stability Zone Detection
papers.summary: Transformer language models contain localized reasoning circuits, contiguous layer blocks that improve reasoning when duplicated at inference time. Finding these circuits currently requires brute-force sweeps costing 25 GPU hours per model. We propose CircuitProbe, which predicts circuit locations f
papers.comment: 11 pages, 1 figure, 3 tables. Code available at https://github.com/agenticclass/circuitprobe
papers.tldr: [research paper] CircuitProbe introduces a CPU-based method to predict reasoning circuits in transformers by detecting statistical signatures in activation dynamics—specifically, stability circuits in early layers (10–25% depth) via the derivative of representation change and magnitude circuits in late layers (85–100% depth) via anomaly scoring—achieving a 3–4 order-of-magnitude speedup over brute-force search. The method requires only 10 calibration examples and generalizes across languages and architectures, with top-1 predictions matching or being within 2 layers of the optimal circuit in all tested models. A scaling study reveals layer duplication improves small models (<3B) by 0.4%–10.0% on reasoning benchmarks but degrades performance in 7B+ models, making it a practical free scaling technique for lightweight LLMs.

papers.title: ATP-Bench: Towards Agentic Tool Planning for MLLM Interleaved Generation
papers.summary: Interleaved text-and-image generation represents a significant frontier for Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), offering a more intuitive way to convey complex information. Current paradigms rely on either image generation or retrieval augmentation, yet they typically treat the two as mutually
pdfUnis: Models (MLLMs), offering a more intuitive way to convey complex information. Cur
papers.tldr: [research paper] ATP-Bench introduces a novel agentic tool planning paradigm for multimodal large language models (MLLMs), where the model autonomously decides when, where, and which tools to invoke to generate interleaved text-image responses. The benchmark includes 7,702 QA/VQA pairs across 8 categories and 25 visual-critical intents, with expert-annotated queries and ground truths, and evaluates tool planning via a multi-agent MLLM-as-a-Judge (MAM) system that assesses precision, missed opportunities, and response quality without requiring end-to-end execution or ground truth. Experiments on 10 state-of-the-art MLLMs reveal poor coherence in interleaved planning, especially in Travel and Renovation tasks, and highlight significant variability in tool-use behavior.

papers.title: Multimodal Models Meet Presentation Attack Detection on ID Documents
papers.summary: The integration of multimodal models into Presentation Attack Detection (PAD) for ID Documents represents a significant advancement in biometric security. Traditional PAD systems rely solely on visual features, which often fail to detect sophisticated spoofing attacks. This study explores the combin
papers.tldr: [research paper] This study evaluates three pretrained multimodal LLMs—PaLiGemma, LLaVA, and Qwen—for Presentation Attack Detection (PAD) on ID documents by assessing their ability to classify bona fide vs. spoofed images using structured prompts. Despite their strong general vision-language capabilities, the models fail to generalize effectively in real-world PAD scenarios, showing poor performance in distinguishing attacks due to insufficient understanding of document-specific fraud patterns and lack of domain-specific knowledge, even when prompted with examples, background context, or recipe-style instructions.

papers.title: Sima AIunty: Caste Audit in LLM-Driven Matchmaking
papers.summary: Social and personal decisions in relational domains such as matchmaking are deeply entwined with cultural norms and historical hierarchies, and can potentially be shaped by algorithmic and AI-mediated assessments of compatibility, acceptance, and stability. In South Asian contexts, caste remains a c
universities: 苏黎世联邦理工学院 (ETH Zurich)

papers.title: SysOM-AI: Continuous Cross-Layer Performance Diagnosis for Production AI Training
papers.summary: Performance diagnosis in production-scale AI training is challenging because subtle OS-level issues can trigger cascading GPU delays and network slowdowns, degrading training efficiency across thousands of GPUs. Existing profiling tools are limited to single system layers, incur prohibitive overhead
papers.comment: 9 pages, 8 figures. Equal contribution by Wenan Mao and Yusheng Zheng
papers.tldr: [research paper] SysOM-AI introduces a production-scale, continuous cross-layer profiling system using eBPF to simultaneously capture CPU call stacks, GPU kernel timings, and NCCL collective events with <0.4% overhead. It enables layered differential diagnosis by comparing anomalous vs. healthy ranks or against baselines, identifying root causes such as GPU thermal throttling or VFS lock contention—issues invisible to single-layer tools—reducing median diagnosis time from days to ~10 minutes across 80,000+ GPUs.

papers.title: Route-Induced Density and Stability (RIDE): Controlled Intervention and Mechanism Analysis of Routing-Style Meta Prompts on LLM Internal States
papers.summary: Routing is widely used to scale large language models, from Mixture-of-Experts gating to multi-model/tool selection. A common belief is that routing to a task ``expert'' activates sparser internal computation and thus yields more certain and stable outputs (the Sparsity--Certainty Hypothesis). We te

papers.title: AI Cosplaying as Astrophysicists: A Controlled Synthetic-Agent Study of AI-Assisted Astrophysical Research Workflows
papers.summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are now widely used in astrophysics, but do they actually make our lives easier, or do they merely invent new physics with enough confidence to hide a minus sign? In a specialized field where checking fluent hallucinations is itself labor-intensive, AI assistance can dem
papers.comment: 16 pages, 8 figures. Began as an April Fools' idea, regrettably became a real methods paper, No language models were physically harmed. GitHub repository of this work: https://github.com/ChunHuangPhy/
pdfUnis: 1Department of Physics and McDonnell Center for the Space Sciences, Washington U
papers.tldr: [experimental study / synthetic-agent simulation] A controlled synthetic-agent experiment simulates 144 astrophysicist profiles performing 2,592 research tasks under four AI assistance policies (cautious, low-verification, verification-heavy, overtrusting), revealing that no AI policy universally outperforms solo work—performance is task-dependent, policy-sensitive, and LLM-specific. Cautious assistance improves on creative and critique tasks but fails catastrophically in derivation-heavy physics, while a rerun with a different LLM (DeepSeek) shows verification-heavy use becomes optimal, eliminating derivation fragility and shifting two policies into high-utility/low-risk zones, demonstrating that AI utility in astrophysics is conditional, workflow-specific, and jointly shaped by agent identity and usage policy.

papers.title: Gen-Searcher: Reinforcing Agentic Search for Image Generation
papers.summary: Recent image generation models have shown strong capabilities in generating high-fidelity and photorealistic images. However, they are fundamentally constrained by frozen internal knowledge, thus often failing on real-world scenarios that are knowledge-intensive or require up-to-date information. In
papers.tldr: [research paper] Gen-Searcher introduces the first trainable multimodal agentic search agent for image generation, enabling multi-hop web search and reasoning to gather external knowledge before synthesis. It constructs two large-scale datasets (SFT-10k, RL-6k) and a new benchmark (KnowGen) for search-grounded image generation, and trains via SFT followed by agentic RL with dual rewards—text-based (prompt quality) and image-based (generation fidelity)—to stabilize learning. The agent achieves +16 points over Qwen-Image on KnowGen and shows strong transferability across backbones, demonstrating superior performance in knowledge-intensive, real-world image generation tasks.

papers.title: Fine-Tuning Large Language Models for Cooperative Tactical Deconfliction of Small Unmanned Aerial Systems
papers.summary: The growing deployment of small Unmanned Aerial Systems (sUASs) in low-altitude airspaces has increased the need for reliable tactical deconfliction under safety-critical constraints. Tactical deconfliction involves short-horizon decision-making in dense, partially observable, and heterogeneous mult
papers.comment: 15 pages, 6 figures, to be published in CVPR 2026 Workshop Proceedings
papers.tldr: [research paper] This work presents a simulation-to-language pipeline that generates rule-consistent, human-aligned tactical deconfliction datasets from the BlueSky air traffic simulator, enabling fine-tuning of LLMs for cooperative sUAS operations. Supervised fine-tuning with LoRA significantly improves decision accuracy, consistency, and separation safety in both offline validation and closed-loop simulations, while preference-based GRPO offers coordination gains at the cost of reduced robustness when interacting with heterogeneous agent policies.

papers.title: Marco DeepResearch: Unlocking Efficient Deep Research Agents via Verification-Centric Design
papers.summary: Deep research agents autonomously conduct open-ended investigations, integrating complex information retrieval with multi-step reasoning across diverse sources to solve real-world problems. To sustain this capability on long-horizon tasks, reliable verification is critical during both training and i
papers.tldr: [research paper] Marco DeepResearch introduces a verification-centric design to address error propagation in deep research agents across three critical stages: (1) QA data synthesis, where graph- and agent-based methods incorporate explicit verification to ensure answer uniqueness, correctness, and controlled difficulty; (2) trajectory construction, where a verifier agent validates sub-task and final answers via web search, enforcing explicit verification patterns in reasoning paths; and (3) test-time scaling, where the agent itself acts as a verifier to iteratively refine responses under a fixed tool budget, enabling efficient performance on challenging queries. The framework achieves superior performance on BrowseComp and BrowseComp-ZH benchmarks, outperforming 8B-scale agents and matching or exceeding several 30B-scale counterparts under a 600-tool-call constraint.

papers.title: CoE: Collaborative Entropy for Uncertainty Quantification in Agentic Multi-LLM Systems
papers.summary: Uncertainty estimation in multi-LLM systems remains largely single-model-centric: existing methods quantify uncertainty within each model but do not adequately capture semantic disagreement across models. To address this gap, we propose Collaborative Entropy (CoE), a unified information-theoretic me
papers.comment: 18 pages, 7 figures, has already published in ICLR workshop "Agentic AI in the Wild: From Hallucinations to Reliable Autonomy"
papers.tldr: [research paper] Collaborative Entropy (CoE) introduces a unified information-theoretic metric to quantify semantic uncertainty in multi-LLM systems by decomposing system-level uncertainty into intra-model aleatoric entropy and inter-model epistemic divergence to an ensemble mean. Unlike existing methods that average per-model uncertainty, CoE explicitly separates and measures semantic disagreement across heterogeneous models, enabling diagnostic insights into whether uncertainty stems from individual model ambiguity or cross-model semantic inconsistency. Experiments on TriviaQA and SQuAD show CoE outperforms baseline entropy and divergence metrics, with performance gains increasing as model diversity grows, demonstrating its effectiveness in capturing residual disagreement that standard methods miss.

papers.title: VulnScout-C: A Lightweight Transformer for C Code Vulnerability Detection
papers.summary: Vulnerability detection in C programs is a critical challenge in software security. Although large language models (LLMs) achieve strong detection performance, their multi-billion-parameter scale makes them impractical for integration into development workflows requiring low latency and continuous a
papers.comment: Submitted to IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
papers.tldr: [research paper] VulnScout-C introduces a 693M-parameter MoE-based transformer derived from Qwen3, optimized for C code vulnerability detection, achieving superior performance (F1=85.4%, 90.0% CWE accuracy) and 4.97ms inference latency on the CASTLE benchmark—outperforming GPT-4-mini and commercial tools. The model is trained on VulnScout, a 33,565-sample dataset generated via a multi-agent pipeline with dual verification (ESBMC + GPT-OSS-120B) to ensure correctness and coverage of underrepresented CWEs, including MITRE Top 25 flaws. A rank-aware loss prioritizes high-severity vulnerabilities, yielding 100% F1 on key CWEs like CWE-78 and CWE-787.

papers.title: Categorical Perception in Large Language Model Hidden States: Structural Warping at Digit-Count Boundaries
papers.summary: Categorical perception (CP) -- enhanced discriminability at category boundaries -- is among the most studied phenomena in perceptual psychology. This paper reports that analogous geometric warping occurs in the hidden-state representations of large language models (LLMs) processing Arabic numerals.
papers.comment: 25 pages, 5 figures, 7 tables. Pre-registered on OSF (osf.io/qrxf3). Code at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/weber-B02C
papers.tldr: [research paper] Large language models exhibit categorical perception (CP) in hidden-state geometry at digit-count boundaries (e.g., 9→10, 99→100), where representational warping—measured via representational similarity analysis—exceeds continuous models by a CP-additive formula (log-distance + boundary boost). This effect is specific to structural discontinuities in tokenisation (e.g., token count jumps), absent in continuous domains like temperature, and manifests in two distinct forms: 'classic CP' (explicit categorisation + warping, e.g., Gemma, Qwen) and 'structural CP' (warping without explicit categorisation, e.g., Llama, Mistral), indicating that architectural discontinuities alone can induce categorical geometry independent of semantic category knowledge.

papers.title: ImagenWorld: Stress-Testing Image Generation Models with Explainable Human Evaluation on Open-ended Real-World Tasks
papers.summary: Advances in diffusion, autoregressive, and hybrid models have enabled high-quality image synthesis for tasks such as text-to-image, editing, and reference-guided composition. Yet, existing benchmarks remain limited, either focus on isolated tasks, cover only narrow domains, or provide opaque scores
papers.tldr: [research paper] ImagenWorld introduces a large-scale, human-explainable benchmark with 3.6K condition sets across six tasks (generation, editing, single/multi-reference) and six domains (art, photorealistic, information graphics, textual graphics, computer graphics, screenshots), revealing that models struggle more in editing—especially local edits—and fail in text-heavy domains like screenshots and information graphics. Closed-source models dominate, while Qwen-Image outperforms via targeted data curation, indicating data design as a key factor. VLM-based metrics achieve Kendall accuracy up to 0.79, matching human ranking, but fail to capture fine-grained, explainable error attribution, underscoring the necessity of human-in-the-loop evaluation for diagnosing failure modes.

papers.title: KazByte: Adapting Qwen models to Kazakh via Byte-level Adapter
papers.summary: Large language models fragment Kazakh text into many more tokens than equivalent English text, because their tokenizers were built for high-resource languages. This tokenizer tax inflates compute, shortens the effective context window, and weakens the model's grip on Kazakh morphology. We propose to
papers.tldr: [research paper] ByteKaz proposes a tokenizer-free approach to adapting Qwen2.5-7B to Kazakh by training a bidirectional byte adapter that maps raw bytes directly into the model’s internal space, bypassing BPE tokenization. The two-stage training process—first learning a byte-to-embedding interface with the model frozen, then fine-tuning only attention layers on Kazakh data—addresses the inefficiency of high token fertility in Kazakh, which inflates sequence length and fragments morphology. Empirical validation on Kazakh benchmarks (e.g., SozKZ, Belebele) is ongoing, with the hypothesis that this method achieves or exceeds the performance of the original BPE-tuned Qwen2.5-7B.

papers.title: Needle in the Repo: A Benchmark for Maintainability in AI-Generated Repository Edits
papers.summary: AI coding agents can now complete complex programming tasks, but existing evaluations largely emphasize behavioral correctness and often overlook maintainability risks such as weak modularity or testability. We present Needle in the Repo (NITR), a diagnostic probe-and-oracle framework for evaluating
papers.tldr: [research paper] NITR introduces a probe-and-oracle framework to evaluate maintainability in AI-generated code edits, revealing that current AI coding agents fail to preserve critical software engineering principles like dependency control and responsibility decomposition—even when behaviorally correct. The framework exposes structural flaws (e.g., 13.3% of cases pass tests but violate maintainability oracles), showing that test-passing alone is insufficient; agent-based methods improve performance marginally (from 28.2% to 45.0%) but do not resolve core architectural failures, indicating a gap between behavioral correctness and sustainable code evolution.

papers.title: A Benchmarking Methodology to Assess Open-Source Video Large Language Models in Automatic Captioning of News Videos
papers.summary: News videos are among the most prevalent content types produced by television stations and online streaming platforms, yet generating textual descriptions to facilitate indexing and retrieval largely remains a manual process. Video Large Language Models (VidLLMs) offer significant potential to autom
universities: 苏黎世联邦理工学院 (ETH Zurich)
pdfUnis: A Benchmarking Methodology to Assess Open-Source
papers.tldr: [research paper] This study evaluates eight open-source Video Large Language Models (VidLLMs) for automatic captioning of news videos using two domain-specific benchmarks: a Chilean TV news corpus (C13) and a BBC News corpus. Standard lexical and semantic metrics fail to capture thematic and entity-level fidelity in news contexts due to surface-form bias and static-frame insensitivity. The paper introduces two novel metrics—Thematic Fidelity Score (TFS) and Entity Fidelity Score (EFS)—that assess thematic structure preservation and named entity coverage, respectively. Results show Gemma 3 outperforms others across all dimensions, highlighting the limitations of existing evaluation methods and establishing a more robust benchmark for news video captioning.

papers.title: The Geometry of Harmful Intent: Training-Free Anomaly Detection via Angular Deviation in LLM Residual Streams
papers.summary: We present LatentBiopsy, a training-free method for detecting harmful prompts by analysing the geometry of residual-stream activations in large language models. Given 200 safe normative prompts, LatentBiopsy computes the leading principal component of their activations at a target layer and characte
papers.comment: 20 pages, 10 figures, 3 tables. Training-free harmful-prompt detector via angular deviation in LLM residual streams. Evaluated on six Qwen variants (base / instruct / abliterated). Achieves AUROC over
papers.tldr: [research paper] LatentBiopsy introduces a training-free anomaly detection method that identifies harmful prompts by measuring angular deviation from the leading principal component of safe normative prompts in LLM residual streams. The method achieves AUROC ≥0.937 across models and remains effective even after refusal mechanisms are surgically removed via orthogonalisation, revealing a geometric dissociation between harmful intent and refusal generation. Harmful prompts exhibit a tightly clustered angular distribution (σθ ≈ 0.03 rad) compared to safe prompts (σθ ≈ 0.27 rad), and model families show opposite ring orientations at the same layer, proving that directional thresholds are unreliable and motivating a symmetric, direction-agnostic scoring rule.

papers.title: Let Triggers Control: Frequency-Aware Dropout for Effective Token Control
papers.summary: Text-to-image models such as Stable Diffusion have achieved unprecedented levels of high-fidelity visual synthesis. As these models advance, personalization of generative models -- commonly facilitated through Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) with a dedicated trigger token -- has become a significant area
papers.comment: CVPR 2026 P13N: Personalization in Generative AI workshop
papers.tldr: [research paper] Frequency-Aware Dropout (FAD) introduces a parameter-free, architecture-agnostic regularization technique that dynamically suppresses gradient flow for tokens frequently co-occurring with a trigger token during training, thereby disentangling style representations and strengthening the trigger token's independent controllability in text-to-image generation. By leveraging co-occurrence statistics and curriculum-inspired scheduling, FAD improves prompt fidelity and stylistic precision without modifying model architecture or adding parameters, effectively solving the entanglement problem in LoRA-based personalization where context cues leak into the trigger token's semantics.

papers.title: Story2Proposal: A Scaffold for Structured Scientific Paper Writing
papers.summary: Generating scientific manuscripts requires maintaining alignment between narrative reasoning, experimental evidence, and visual artifacts across the document lifecycle. Existing language-model generation pipelines rely on unconstrained text synthesis with validation applied only after generation, of
papers.tldr: [research paper] Story2Proposal introduces a contract-governed multi-agent system that enforces structural and visual consistency in scientific paper generation by maintaining a persistent shared visual contract tracked across architect, writer, refiner, and renderer agents. The generate–evaluate–adapt loop uses evaluation agents to update the contract state, ensuring claim-evidence alignment, section coherence, and visual artifact fidelity; experiments on Jericho-derived tasks show a 2.182-point improvement over DirectChat and a 0.508-point gain over Fars in expert evaluation scores, demonstrating superior structural integrity and cross-section consistency.

papers.title: HandVQA: Diagnosing and Improving Fine-Grained Spatial Reasoning about Hands in Vision-Language Models
papers.summary: Understanding the fine-grained articulation of human hands is critical in high-stakes settings such as robot-assisted surgery, chip manufacturing, and AR/VR-based human-AI interaction. Despite achieving near-human performance on general vision-language benchmarks, current vision-language models (VLM
papers.comment: Accepted in CVPR 2026; Project page, code, and dataset: https://kcsayem.github.io/handvqa/
papers.tldr: [research paper] HandVQA introduces a large-scale diagnostic benchmark that evaluates vision-language models' fine-grained spatial reasoning about hands through structured visual question answering, leveraging 3D joint annotations from FreiHAND, InterHand2.6M, and FPHA. The benchmark dissects hand pose into five subtasks—joint angles, distances, and relative positions along X/Y/Z axes—and reveals systematic failures in VLMs, including hallucinated finger parts and incorrect geometric interpretations. Fine-tuning via LoRA on HandVQA enables zero-shot transfer of 3D spatial knowledge, improving gesture recognition (+10.33%) and hand-object interaction (+2.63%) without task-specific training.

papers.title: From Pixels to BFS: High Maze Accuracy Does Not Imply Visual Planning
papers.summary: How do multimodal models solve visual spatial tasks -- through genuine planning, or through brute-force search in token space? We introduce \textsc{MazeBench}, a benchmark of 110 procedurally generated maze images across nine controlled groups, and evaluate 16 model configurations from OpenAI, Anthr
papers.comment: 15 pages, 10 figures. Code and mazes available at https://github.com/alrod97/LLMs_mazes

papers.title: Evaluating Small Language Models for Front-Door Routing: A Harmonized Benchmark and Synthetic-Traffic Experiment
papers.summary: Selecting the appropriate model at inference time -- the routing problem -- requires jointly optimizing output quality, cost, latency, and governance constraints. Existing approaches delegate this decision to LLM-based classifiers or preference-trained routers that are themselves costly and high-lat
papers.comment: 23 pages, 1 figure, 9 tables. Article 8 in the TAAC Research Series. Code and data: https://github.com/micoverde/plexor-slm-frontdoor-rct
papers.tldr: [research paper] A controlled evaluation demonstrates that small language models (SLMs) with 1–4B parameters can perform front-door routing with sub-second latency and zero marginal cost, enabling multi-objective optimization of output quality, latency, and cost. A harmonized benchmark and randomized experiment show that Qwen-2.5-3B achieves Pareto-dominant performance (0.793 accuracy, 988 ms median latency, $0 marginal cost), outperforming larger models in cost-efficiency, while DeepSeek-V3 fails latency constraints; no model meets standalone viability (≥0.85 accuracy, ≤2,000 ms P95), indicating that SLM-based routing is viable in practice but still limited by accuracy gaps and untested downstream quality translation.

papers.title: Prune as You Generate: Online Rollout Pruning for Faster and Better RLVR
papers.summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has significantly advanced the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs). However, methods such as GRPO and DAPO suffer from substantial computational cost, since they rely on sampling many rollouts for each prompt. Moreover, in RLVR
papers.tldr: [research paper] ARRoL introduces an online rollout pruning method that dynamically prunes partial rollouts during generation using a lightweight quality head to predict success probability, enabling early pruning for reduced computational cost and improved reward balance. The method enhances learning signals by ensuring within-group reward diversity, achieving +2.30 to +2.99 accuracy gains over GRPO/DAPO on Qwen-3 and LLaMA-3.2 models, with up to 1.7× training speedup and +8.33 accuracy gains in test-time scaling via weighted voting.

papers.title: IntentWeave: A Progressive Entry Ladder for Multi-Surface Browser Agents in Cloud Portals
papers.summary: Browser agents built on LLMs can act in web interfaces, yet most remain confined to a single chat surface (e.g., a sidebar). This mismatch with real browsing can increase context-switching and reduce user control. We introduce \textbf{IntentWeave}, a design space of ten spatial paradigms for embeddi
papers.tldr: [research paper] IntentWeave introduces a design space of ten spatial paradigms for multi-surface browser agent entry, organized into a progressive ladder from micro-interventions to dedicated workspaces. The framework enables dynamic escalation and retreat based on user behavior, balancing efficiency, perceived control, and flow; user studies show that a mixed sidecar approach achieves optimal satisfaction by combining subtle in-situ cues with accessible workspace support, while avoiding disruptive takeovers or insufficient assistance.

papers.title: DeepStock: Reinforcement Learning with Policy Regularizations for Inventory Management
papers.summary: Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) provides a general-purpose methodology for training inventory policies that can leverage big data and compute. However, off-the-shelf implementations of DRL have seen mixed success, often plagued by high sensitivity to the hyperparameters used during training. In th
universities: 苏黎世联邦理工学院 (ETH Zurich)
papers.tldr: [research paper] DeepStock introduces policy regularizations grounded in classical inventory theory—specifically base stock and feature coefficient structures—to constrain DRL policy outputs in inventory management. These regularizations transform black-box DRL decisions into interpretable, demand-sensitive order quantities by enforcing functional forms that align with inventory logic, reducing hyperparameter sensitivity, accelerating convergence, and enabling full-scale deployment across 1M+ SKU-warehouse combinations at Alibaba’s Tmall platform. Synthetic and real-world experiments show that regularized policies outperform vanilla DRL methods in both stability and performance, challenging the notion that off-the-shelf DRL is optimal for inventory control.

papers.title: Implicit Grading Bias in Large Language Models: How Writing Style Affects Automated Assessment Across Math, Programming, and Essay Tasks
papers.summary: As large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as automated graders in educational settings, concerns about fairness and bias in their evaluations have become critical. This study investigates whether LLMs exhibit implicit grading bias based on writing style when the underlying content co
papers.tldr: [empirical research paper] This study reveals that large language models exhibit significant implicit grading bias based on writing style—particularly in essay tasks—despite explicit instructions to evaluate content correctness only. Using a controlled dataset of 180 student responses across math, programming, and writing, with perturbations like informal language and non-native phrasing, the authors find statistically significant penalties (Cohen’s d from 0.64 to 4.25) for stylistic deviations, especially in writing, where informal language incurs average deductions of 1.90 (LLaMA) and 1.20 (Qwen) points—equivalent to grade-level drops. In contrast, math and programming show minimal bias, indicating that bias is subject-dependent and persists even with prompt-level counter-bias instructions, challenging the efficacy of current prompt engineering for equitable grading.

papers.title: OpenSeeker: Democratizing Frontier Search Agents by Fully Open-Sourcing Training Data
papers.summary: Deep search capabilities have become an indispensable competency for frontier Large Language Model (LLM) agents, yet the development of high-performance search agents remains dominated by industrial giants due to a lack of transparent, high-quality training data. This persistent data scarcity has fu
papers.tldr: [research paper] OpenSeeker introduces a fully open-source search agent that achieves frontier-level performance using only supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on 11.7k synthesized samples, enabled by two key innovations: (1) fact-grounded, scalable, and controllable QA synthesis via topological graph expansion and entity obfuscation to generate multi-hop reasoning tasks rooted in real web structure; (2) denoised trajectory synthesis using retrospective summarization to clean agent histories while preserving raw trajectory supervision, enabling robust information extraction. The agent outperforms existing open-source models like DeepDive and rivals industrial competitors such as Tongyi DeepResearch on benchmarks like BrowseComp and BrowseComp-ZH, demonstrating that high-quality, transparent training data can democratize advanced search agent development.

papers.title: Logics-Parsing-Omni Technical Report
papers.summary: Addressing the challenges of fragmented task definitions and the heterogeneity of unstructured data in multimodal parsing, this paper proposes the Omni Parsing framework. This framework establishes a Unified Taxonomy covering documents, images, and audio-visual streams, introducing a progressive par
papers.tldr: [technical report] The Omni Parsing framework introduces a three-level progressive paradigm—Holistic Detection, Fine-grained Recognition, and Multi-level Interpreting—to unify perception and cognition across documents, images, and audio-visual streams. It enforces evidence anchoring between low-level observations and high-level semantics, enabling verifiable, traceable knowledge extraction. The Logics-Parsing-Omni model leverages structured, entity-rich training data with optimized annotations for layout, temporal grounding, and attribute extraction, achieving superior performance on OmniParsingBench through synergistic integration of spatial-temporal grounding and logical reasoning.

papers.title: How Trustworthy Are LLM-as-Judge Ratings for Interpretive Responses? Implications for Qualitative Research Workflows

papers.tldr: [research paper] MASQuant introduces Modality-Aware Smoothing (MAS) to resolve smoothing misalignment in multimodal large language models (MLLMs), where disparate activation magnitudes across modalities cause over-smoothing of non-dominant signals. papers.tldr: [research paper] The paper adapts DeepSeek-OCR-2 for Optical Chemical Structure Recognition (OCSR) by framing it as image-conditioned SMILES generation. A two-stage progressive fine-tuning strategy—starting with LoRA-based parameter-efficient adaptation and transitioning to split-learning-rate full-parameter fine-tuning—overcomes training instability.
